docs.title: How These Notes Are Made
docs.text:
These notes are generated through an automated pipeline:

  1. Recording — Class recordings are uploaded to Google Drive by the course coordinators
  2. Transcription — Audio is transcribed using OpenAI Whisper (large-v3-turbo model) with local GPU acceleration
  3. Note Generation — Transcripts are processed by Claude (Anthropic) to produce structured study notes following subject-specific templates
  4. Publishing — The site is built with MkDocs using the Material theme and hosted on GitHub Pages

Each session's notes include a session overview, key themes, detailed explanations, Arabic vocabulary with transliterations, and practical takeaways. Notes are AI-generated and may contain inaccuracies — always verify against primary sources and your own class notes.

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This introductory session emphasized the importance and scope of Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) as a comprehensive way of life. The instructor discussed the generational nature of Islamic knowledge, the significance of sincere intention in seeking knowledge, and introduced the fundamental structure of fiqh. The session covered the two main categories of Islamic law and outlined the five pillars of Islam that will form the core curriculum for the semester.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Generational Impact of Islamic Knowledge: How learning and teaching Islamic knowledge benefits entire family lineages
  • Sincerity in Seeking Knowledge: The importance of studying for Allah's sake alone, distinguishing between fame (shohra) and divine acceptance (kabuliyah)
  • Comprehensive Nature of Fiqh: Islam as a complete way of life encompassing all aspects of human behavior
  • Priority of Divine Obligations: Allah's rights take precedence over worldly matters
  • Foundation for Spiritual Success: Knowledge of fiqh as preparation for the hereafter

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Fiqh Islamic jurisprudence; understanding of Islamic law Ibadat Acts of worship Mu'amalat Social transactions and interpersonal relations Shohra Fame (bottom-up system) Kabuliyah Divine acceptance (top-down from Allah) Tahara Purification/cleanliness Badani Physical worship requiring bodily effort Mali Worship requiring financial expenditure Hafidh One who has memorized the Quran Istiqamah Steadfastness and consistency

docs.title: Definition and Scope of Fiqh
docs.text:
• Fiqh encompasses both ibadat (worship) and mu'amalat (social dealings)
  • Books of fiqh are expansive because Islam governs all aspects of life
  • Both categories are essential for a complete Islamic lifestyle

docs.title: Hadith of Five Pillars
docs.text: Evidence: Abdullah ibn Umar (ra) narrated that the Prophet ﷺ said: "Islam has been built on five pillars: testifying that there is no deity worthy of worship except Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, establishing salah, paying zakah, making hajj to the house of Allah, and fasting in the month of Ramadan." [Bukhari & Muslim]

docs.title: Quranic Evidence for Priority System
docs.text: Surah Al-Jumu'ah (62:9-10): - When called for Jumu'ah prayer: "Run towards the remembrance of Allah and leave your businesses" - After prayer completion: "Spread out in the earth" (resume worldly activities) - Ruling: Divine obligations always take priority over worldly matters

docs.title: Categories of Worship (Ibadat)
docs.text:
1. Badani - Physical worship requiring bodily effort
  2. Mali - Worship requiring financial expenditure
  3. Mixed - Combining both physical and financial elements (e.g., Hajj)

docs.title: Tahara as Prerequisite
docs.text:
• Hadith: "Al-tahuru shatru al-iman" (Cleanliness is half of faith)
  • Required for: Salah, Tawaf, touching/reciting Quran
  • Certain states prevent mosque entry and Quran recitation

docs.title: Hajj Classification
docs.text:
• Considered both Badani and Mali worship
  • Requires physical presence and financial capability
  • Historical requirement: ensuring family's financial security before departure
  • Modern costs range from $6,000-$25,000 per person

docs.title: Nikah (Marriage) Classification
docs.text:
• Contains elements of both ibadat and mu'amalat
  • Prophetic practice (Sunnah) aspect makes it worship
  • Daily marital relations and rights fall under social dealings

docs.title: For Students of Knowledge
docs.text:
1. Maintain Sincere Intention: Seek knowledge purely for Allah's sake
  2. Understand Generational Impact: Your learning benefits your entire family lineage
  3. Embrace Challenges: Hard work is necessary to overcome initial difficulties
  4. Seek Divine Acceptance: Aim for kabuliyah (divine acceptance) rather than shohra (fame)

docs.title: Semester Curriculum Plan
docs.text: The course will cover the five pillars in order: 1. Tahara (Purification) - Prerequisite for worship 2. Salah (Prayer) 3. Sawm (Fasting) 4. Zakah (Alms tax) 5. Hajj (Pilgrimage)

docs.title: Study Approach
docs.text:
• Each academic year builds upon the previous one's foundation
  • Consistent effort and dedication are essential for progression
  • Arabic proficiency will develop throughout the program
  • Students should actively seek help when facing difficulties

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session was a review of the introductory material to Fiqh, covering fundamental definitions, categories, and practical applications. The instructor emphasized reviewing previous material before continuing with new content. The session included a brief Q&A about wudu (ablution) practices, particularly regarding washing hands, dealing with dog saliva on skin vs. clothing, and washing the face/beard area during ablution.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Definition and scope of Fiqh according to different classical scholars
  • Categories of Islamic law: Ibadah (worship) and Mu'amalat (transactions)
  • Subcategories of worship: physical (badaniyya) and financial (maliyya)
  • Practical application of ablution rules
  • Preparation for comprehensive Fiqh study

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Fiqh Islamic jurisprudence; understanding religious rulings Istilah Terminological definition Ibadah Acts of worship Mu'amalat Transactions/dealings between people Badaniyya Physical acts of worship Maliyya Financial acts of worship Tahara Purification Salah Prayer Sawm Fasting Zakah Obligatory charity/alms tax Hajj Annual pilgrimage Wudu Ablution Bayr Buying and selling Sadaqah Voluntary charity Masail Religious issues/rulings

docs.title: Definition of Fiqh (Three Scholarly Perspectives):
docs.text:
1. Imam Abu Hanifa (rahimahullah): "Fiqh is a knowledge of a person pertaining to the rulings of what is good and bad for him"

  2. Imam Shafi (rahimahullah): "Fiqh is the knowledge of religious practical rulings deduced from the detailed sources"

  3. Majalla (Ottoman Legal Code): Defined as matters relating to religious practical affairs

docs.title: Wudu Rulings Discussed:
docs.text:
• Hands are washed at the beginning of wudu and are included in the washing of arms up to the elbows
  • For tight rings: must be removed or ensure water reaches underneath
  • For loose rings: sufficient if certain that water penetrates

docs.title: Primary Categories of Fiqh:
docs.text:
1. Ibadah (Worship)
  2. Badaniyya (Physical): Acts requiring physical exertion and activity

  3. Maliyya (Financial): Acts involving financial obligations (zakah, sadaqah, financial aspects of hajj)

  4. Mu'amalat (Transactions): Daily dealings including buying, selling, contracts, oaths, and agreements

docs.title: Core Subjects to be Covered:
docs.text:
• Tahara (Purification) - prerequisite for many acts of worship
  • Salah (Prayer)
  • Sawm (Fasting)
  • Zakah (Alms tax)
  • Hajj (Annual pilgrimage)

docs.title: Washing After Dog Contact:
docs.text:
• For skin: Seven times (established ruling)
  • For clothing: Three times (instructor needs to verify exact number, noting some hadith mention seven)

docs.title: Beard Washing in Wudu:
docs.text:
• Thick beard: Wash only the hair directly connected to the face, not the hanging portions
  • Thin beard: Water must reach the underlying skin of the face and chin

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
1. Comprehensive Nature of Fiqh: Islamic jurisprudence encompasses all aspects of life, not just the five pillars of worship, but extends to all daily transactions and interactions

  2. Practical Wudu Guidelines:

  3. Ensure water reaches all required areas including under loose jewelry
  4. Different rules apply for thick vs. thin facial hair

  5. Proper washing technique varies based on the area of the body

  6. Study Methodology: Regular review of fundamental concepts is essential for building comprehensive understanding of Islamic law

  7. Integration of Theory and Practice: Understanding definitions must be coupled with practical application in daily religious observances

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered a comprehensive review of the four farāʾiḍ (obligations) of wudu and then proceeded to the emphasized sunan (recommended acts) and adab (etiquette) of wudu. The class began with individual questioning to test students' understanding of water types permissible for purification and the requirements for washing different body parts during wudu. The instructor then introduced 17 emphasized sunan of wudu and 8 mustaḥabbāt (preferred etiquettes), explaining the practical application and flexibility of these recommendations based on circumstances.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Distinction between obligations and recommendations: Clear differentiation between farāʾiḍ (must be done), sunan muʾakkadah (emphasized recommendations), and mustaḥabbāt (preferred etiquette)
  • Practical flexibility in worship: Sunan and mustaḥabbāt can be omitted when water is limited or when following them would cause difficulty or harm
  • Comprehensive purification: Beyond the basic requirements, Islam encourages thorough and mindful purification with specific techniques and sequences
  • Natural water sources: Emphasis on understanding what constitutes natural vs. artificial alterations to water

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Farāʾiḍ Obligations/obligatory acts Sunan Recommended acts (plural of sunnah) Sunnah Muʾakkadah Emphasized sunnah Mustaḥabbāt Preferred/recommended acts Adab Etiquette Khilāl Running wet fingers through beard or between fingers/toes Miswāk Natural tooth stick for cleaning teeth Masḥ Wiping (especially of the head) Ghalib Majority/predominant Maʿdhūr One with chronic excuse

docs.title: Water Permissibility Rules
docs.text:
• Natural alterations are permissible: Seawater (salty), pond water (murky), well water with mineral taste - all remain valid for wudu as these are natural characteristics
  • Artificial alterations invalidate: Rose water, heavily chlorinated pool water - invalid because external substances have artificially altered the water's qualities
  • Two-quality rule: If two of the three qualities (color, taste, smell) are altered, water becomes invalid for wudu

docs.title: Thick vs. Thin Beard Ruling
docs.text:
• Thin beard: Water must reach the skin of the chin
  • Thick beard: Water must reach the hair connected to the skin, but doesn't need to penetrate to the actual skin of the chin
  • Sunnah method: Perform khilāl (running wet fingers through the beard) after washing the face

docs.title: Head Wiping Definition
docs.text:
• Legal definition: Only hair growing above the ears counts as "head"
  • Minimum requirement: One-fourth of the head area
  • Hair below ears: Cannot count toward the masḥ requirement, even if tied above the head

docs.title: The 17 Emphasized Sunan of Wudu:
docs.text:
1. Intention (niyyah)
  2. Using miswāk
  3. Saying Bismillah
  4. Washing hands to wrists initially
  5. Maintaining correct order of limbs
  6. Continuity (not letting previous limb dry before washing next)
  7. Washing limbs three times each
  8. Rinsing mouth three times
  9. Rinsing soft part of nose three times
  10. Khilāl of the beard
  11. Khilāl between fingers and toes
  12. Wiping entire head once and ears with same water
  13. Rubbing limbs when washing
  14. Starting with right limb for arms and feet
  15. Starting from fingertips/toes when washing
  16. Starting from front when wiping head
  17. Wiping back of neck (mandūb, not sunnah muʾakkadah)

docs.title: The 8 Etiquettes (Adab) of Wudu:
docs.text:
1. Facing qibla (when practically possible)
  2. Avoiding water splashing on body/clothes
  3. Making prescribed duʿās
  4. Mentioning Allah's name with each limb
  5. Performing wudu independently
  6. Performing before prayer time (unless maʿdhūr)
  7. Reciting shahādah after completion
  8. Drinking leftover water

docs.title: Miswāk Usage
docs.text:
• Prophetic saying: "As-siwāku mitharatun lil-fami wa murḍātun lil-rabbi" (The miswāk purifies the mouth and pleases Allah)
  • Toothbrush ruling: Does not substitute for miswāk in fulfilling the sunnah
  • Proper holding: Between fingers with pinky support from behind

docs.title: Neck Wiping Classification
docs.text:
• Difference of opinion noted: Some sources classify wiping back of neck as sunnah muʾakkadah, others as mandūb
  • Method: Use back of fingers, no new water required
  • Area: Flat part of neck only, not the throat or curved areas

docs.title: Flexibility in Practice
docs.text:
• When water is limited, prioritize farāʾiḍ over sunan
  • When following adab would cause harm or difficulty (e.g., facing qibla in poorly designed wudu areas), safety takes precedence
  • Practical considerations allow for adaptation while maintaining the spirit of the practice

docs.title: Daily Implementation
docs.text:
• Continuity: Don't let limbs dry between washing
  • Order matters for reward: Following sequence increases spiritual benefit
  • Thoroughness: Khilāl ensures complete coverage of beard, fingers, and toes
  • Mindfulness: Dhikr during wudu maintains focus on worship rather than routine

docs.title: Water Conservation
docs.text:
• Sunan can be omitted when water conservation is necessary
  • The practice encourages efficiency while maintaining spiritual mindfulness
  • Natural water sources are preferred and generally permissible even with natural variations

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the disliked actions (makruh) during wudu, the legal reasons for performing wudu, conditions for validity, and categories of wudu (obligatory, mandatory, recommended). The class also included a review of the emphasized sunnah of wudu from the previous session and concluded with a discussion on the differences between fard and wajib rulings.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Classification of disliked actions in Islamic law (makruh tahrimi vs makruh tanzihi)
  • Proper etiquette and conditions for valid wudu
  • Legal reasoning (shar'i) for performing wudu
  • Categories of when wudu is required: obligatory, mandatory, and recommended
  • Understanding the hierarchy of Islamic legal rulings (fard, wajib, sunnah)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Makruh tahrimi Prohibitively disliked - mandatory to abstain from Makruh tanzihi Mildly disliked - better to avoid but no sin Isa'ah Doing something wrong (between the two types of makruh) Israf Wastage, using excessive resources Shar'i Legal reasoning from Islamic perspective Rukun (pl. arkan) Integral/pillar of an act Fard Obligatory act - if omitted, entire action becomes invalid Wajib Mandatory act - sin if omitted intentionally, but action can be remedied Mandub/Mustahab Recommended/preferred action Ma'dhur Person with chronic excuse that persists Sajdah al-sahu Prostration of forgetfulness to remedy missed wajib acts

docs.title: Disliked Actions During Wudu
docs.text:
Five main disliked actions:

  1. Wasting water (israf) - Using more than necessary, including washing more than three times
  2. Using too little water - Insufficient to reach all required body parts
  3. Striking/slapping face with water - Causes excessive water on clothing (makruh tanzihi)
  4. Speaking during wudu - Except for dhikr, du'a, or necessary matters (responding to parents, salam)
  5. Having assistance without valid excuse - When capable of performing wudu independently

Evidence cited: "Indeed, the wasteful are brothers of the devils" (Qur'anic reference to prohibition of waste)

docs.title: Legal Reasons for Performing Wudu
docs.text: Two primary shar'i reasons: 1. Desire to perform acts impermissible without wudu (prayer, touching Qur'an) 2. Divine command due to near expiration of prayer time

docs.title: Conditions for Legal Responsibility (Taklif)
docs.text:
1. Being Muslim
  2. Puberty (bulugh)
  3. Sanity (aql)
  4. Termination of menstruation, postnatal bleeding, or ritual impurity
  5. Access to sufficient purifying water
  6. Physical ability to perform prayer

docs.title: Conditions for Validity of Wudu
docs.text:
1. Complete water contact - Water must reach skin of all obligatory limbs
  2. Barrier removal - No substances (paint, glue) blocking water from skin
  3. No contradictory states - Nothing breaking wudu during its performance

docs.title: Categories of Wudu
docs.text:
1. Obligatory (Fard) - When in minor ritual impurity and intending: - Ritual prayer (salah) - Prostration of recital (sajdah al-tilawah) - Touching Qur'anic verses (unless with non-attached barrier)

2. Mandatory (Wajib) - When in minor ritual impurity and intending: - Circumambulation (tawaf) - Touching books of tafsir

3. Recommended (Mandub) - When in minor ritual impurity for: - Touching fiqh books (out of respect) - Renewing wudu for another prayer - Avoiding scholarly disagreement (after touching opposite gender/genitalia) - Before sleeping - After waking from sleep - After committing sins - After laughing loudly outside prayer

docs.title: Barriers and Substances
docs.text:
• Body lotions: Permissible as oils don't create barriers
  • Vaseline: Questionable if it forms barrier layer
  • Phone apps: Difference of opinion regarding touching digital Qur'an

docs.title: Attached vs Non-attached Barriers
docs.text:
• Attached barriers (impermissible): Gloves, book covers
  • Non-attached barriers (permissible): Cloth held in hand, head covering used to grab Qur'an

docs.title: Key Differences: Fard vs Wajib
docs.text:
• Fard: If omitted, entire act becomes invalid; denying established fard can remove one from Islam
  • Wajib: If omitted intentionally, incurs sin but act can be remedied (e.g., through sajdah al-sahu); denying doesn't remove from Islam

docs.title: Practical Guidance
docs.text:
• Clothing length: Men's garments must not extend below ankles (except Abu Bakr's specific exception)
  • Speaking during wudu: Permissible for religious matters (salam responses, dhikr) but avoid worldly conversation
  • Assistance: Acceptable for holding water sources, not for performing actual washing without need
  • Supplications in restroom: Can be made silently in heart when necessary

docs.title: Etiquette Reminders
docs.text:
• Maintain proper order of limbs during wudu
  • Ensure continuity between washing actions
  • Start with right limbs when applicable
  • Use appropriate amount of water (neither wasteful nor insufficient)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on the nullifiers of wudu and the obligations and methods of ghusl (ritual bath). The instructor covered nine specific actions that invalidate wudu, ranging from bodily emissions to loss of consciousness. The class then transitioned to ghusl requirements, detailing both the minimum obligatory (fard) method and the complete sunnah method. Three categories of ghusl were discussed: obligatory (fard), mandatory (wajib), and recommended (nafl). The session concluded with clarifications on situations that do NOT require ghusl and addressed numerous practical questions from students.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Distinction between minor ritual impurity (requiring wudu) and major ritual impurity (requiring ghusl)
  • Detailed understanding of what constitutes "exiting" from different body openings
  • The concept of istibra and istinja for males
  • Categories of ghusl and their respective requirements
  • Practical applications of fiqh rulings in daily life
  • The concept of ma'zur (chronically excused person)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Janabah State of major ritual impurity Ghusl Ritual bath for purification from major impurity Istinja Cleaning the private parts after using the restroom Istibra Ensuring complete removal of fluids (specific to males) Madhi Clear fluid that exits during arousal (breaks wudu only) Wadi Droplets of urine after using restroom (breaks wudu only) Mani Sexual fluid whose emission requires ghusl Ma'zur Person with chronic excuse (e.g., incontinence) Amal Kathir Excessive movement that invalidates prayer Qaha qaha Laughing out loud (breaks both wudu and salah) Niyyah Intention Khilal Passing fingers through beard during wudu

docs.title: Nullifiers of Wudu (Nine Categories):
docs.text:
1. Anything exiting from the two openings - Even appearance at the tip is considered exiting
  2. Filth flowing from other openings - Must flow beyond point of exit; mere appearance at surface doesn't break wudu
  3. Vomiting a mouthful or more - Amount that cannot be contained without strain
  4. Sleeping without buttocks firmly planted - Lying down or leaning invalidates wudu
  5. Loss of consciousness
  6. Insanity - Loss of perception of reality
  7. Drunkenness
  8. Laughing out loud during salah by adults - Breaks both wudu and salah
  9. Contact between erect male and female private parts - Direct contact nullifies wudu

docs.title: Obligatory Elements of Ghusl (Minimum Requirements):
docs.text:
1. Rinsing mouth entirely once
  2. Rinsing nose up to the bone once
  3. Washing entire body once without undue difficulty

docs.title: Sunnah Method of Ghusl (In Order):
docs.text:
1. Making intention (niyyah)
  2. Washing hands to wrists
  3. Washing private parts front and back
  4. Washing off any filth from body
  5. Performing complete wudu
  6. Pouring water over entire body three times, starting with head

docs.title: Categories of Ghusl:
docs.text:
1. Obligatory (Fard) - Required when: - Emission of sexual fluid (mani) with or without pleasure - Penetration of male private part into female private part or back area - End of menstruation or postnatal bleeding

2. Mandatory (Wajib): - Person becoming Muslim while in state of major impurity - (Note: Sounder opinion considers this still fard)

3. Recommended (Nafl) - For: - Friday prayer (Jumu'ah) - Two Eid prayers - Entering state of ihram - Day of Arafah - Entering Makkah or Madinah - Visiting the Prophet ﷺ

docs.title: Ma'zur (Chronically Excused) Conditions:
docs.text:
• Excuse must persist for entire prayer time
  • Person makes wudu at beginning of each prayer time
  • Wudu remains valid until that prayer time ends
  • Can pray even if excuse occurs during salah

docs.title: Imam Abu Hanifa's Position:
docs.text:
• Female vaginal discharge is not considered impure but categorized as similar to sweat
  • Does not require ghusl but may require wudu depending on circumstances

docs.title: Beard Washing Differences:
docs.text:
• In wudu: Thickness of beard determines washing method
  • In ghusl: All beards require water to reach the skin regardless of thickness

docs.title: Movement During Salah:
docs.text:
• Amal Kathir (excessive movement) determined by whether person appears to be praying from distance
  • If observer cannot tell person is praying due to movement, salah is invalid

docs.title: For Males - Restroom Etiquette:
docs.text:
• Must perform istibra before istinja
  • Istibra ensures complete removal of fluids through pressure methods (coughing, standing, tissue placement)
  • Prevents later invalidation of wudu from residual fluids

docs.title: During Salah Situations:
docs.text:
• If wudu breaks during congregational prayer, exit carefully (preferably during sajda)
  • If leading prayer and wudu breaks, appoint deputy to continue
  • Laughing during prayer: Silent laughter breaks salah only; loud laughter breaks both salah and wudu

docs.title: Medical Considerations:
docs.text:
• Chronic conditions (frequent gas, incontinence) fall under ma'zur ruling
  • Temporary dietary issues don't constitute chronic excuse
  • Person can delay prayer within time limit to manage temporary conditions

docs.title: Fasting Considerations:
docs.text:
• When performing ghusl while fasting, be careful with mouth and nose rinsing
  • Avoid excessive water that might enter throat/stomach

docs.title: Mixed Gathering Guidelines:
docs.text:
• Accidental contact doesn't break wudu through touch
  • Proper Islamic etiquette should prevent such situations

docs.title: Blood and Wound Care:
docs.text:
• Blood appearing at wound surface without flowing doesn't break wudu
  • Blood-stained clothing from non-flowing blood remains pure
  • Once blood flows from its place, wudu is broken and area becomes impure

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered two major topics in Islamic jurisprudence: Tayammum (dry ablution) and wiping over foot coverings (Khuf). The lesson detailed the conditions, procedures, and rulings for both practices as alternatives to regular wudu when circumstances require them. The class concluded with a review quiz covering previous lessons on ghusl and wudu.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Tayammum as a substitute for wudu when water is unavailable or inaccessible
  • The three obligatory components of tayammum: intention, striking earth and wiping face, striking earth and wiping arms
  • Conditions and time limits for wiping over khuf (foot coverings)
  • The principle of reasonable likelihood in determining valid excuses
  • Distinction between prayers with qada (makeup) and those without

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Tayammum Dry ablution Mil shar'i (ميل شرعي) Legal mile (approximately 1.16 miles) Janabah Major ritual impurity Qada Makeup prayer Khilal Running wet fingers through beard/between fingers Khuf Leather socks or foot coverings Ma'zur Person with continuous excuse (uncontrollable condition) Masalah Jurisprudential issue Faqid al-tahurain Loss/absence of the two purifying agents (water and earth)

docs.title: Tayammum Permissibility
docs.text: Tayammum is valid when there is a legitimate excuse: 1. Distance from water: Roughly 1.16 miles (1 legal mile) or 1.86 kilometers away 2. Extreme cold: Risk of frostbite or limb damage with no means to heat water 3. Sickness: Where water use would worsen illness or prolong recovery 4. Wounds on most of body: Majority of body has wounds 5. Fear of thirst: Risk of dehydration from using available water 6. Fear of enemy or wild beast: Water available but inaccessible due to danger 7. Lack of apparatus: No means to extract water (rope, bucket, etc.) 8. Fear of missing funeral/Eid prayer: Risk of missing prayers without qada

docs.title: Tayammum Procedure
docs.text: Three obligatory components: 1. Intention - to remove state of impurity or enable prayer 2. First strike: Strike earth, wipe entire face 3. Second strike: Strike earth, wipe arms up to and including elbows

docs.title: Khuf (Foot Covering) Rules
docs.text:
• Valid for both residents (24 hours) and travelers (72 hours)
  • Time period begins when wudu is nullified, not when khuf is worn
  • Must be worn in state of ritual purity
  • Can wash feet first, put on khuf, then complete wudu

docs.title: Tayammum Substances
docs.text: Permitted: Any pure substance of earth that is: - Not malleable (excludes metals) - Cannot burn to ashes (excludes wood, plants) - Examples: dirt, sand, limestone, stones, dust on clothes (if visible after rubbing)

docs.title: Khuf Conditions
docs.text: Must be: 1. Made of thick material (wool, broadcloth, etc.) 2. Stay on foot without external tying 3. Not see-through 4. Cover foot up to and including ankles 5. Able to walk 3.48 miles without tearing 6. Water should not seep through when wiping

docs.title: Invalidation of Khuf Wiping
docs.text:
1. Anything that nullifies wudu
  2. Removing even one khuf
  3. Expiration of time period (unless extreme cold conditions)
  4. Most of foot leaving the khuf

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
• Forgetting water availability: If one performs tayammum forgetting they had water, prayers don't need to be repeated upon remembering
  • Imitation prayer: When neither water nor earth substances are available, one performs prayer motions without intention, then makes up the prayer later (masalah of faqid al-tahurain)
  • Wiping area: Minimum area equivalent to three smallest fingers must be wiped on top of each khuf
  • Hole tolerance: Holes smaller than the area of three smallest toes are permissible

docs.title: Contemporary Applications
docs.text:
• Using GPS/maps to determine water availability while traveling
  • Understanding that multiple Eid jamaats may affect tayammum rulings
  • Colostomy bag patients fall under ma'zur ruling for wudu
  • Modern waterproof socks can qualify as valid khuf if meeting all conditions

docs.title: Practical Scenarios Discussed
docs.text:
• Traffic delays during Eid with crowded wudu facilities
  • Extreme weather conditions and water heating capabilities
  • Medical conditions like arthritis affecting water use
  • Breaking wudu during prayer and rejoining congregation

docs.title: Key Principles
docs.text:
• Reasonable likelihood standard for determining valid excuses
  • Priority of maintaining prayer times while seeking proper purification
  • Flexibility in Islamic law for genuine hardship situations
  • Importance of intention in all acts of worship

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the rulings for wiping over casts and bandages during wudu, followed by a comprehensive discussion of menstruation (hayd), postnatal bleeding (nifas), and dysfunctional uterine bleeding (istihada). The class detailed the conditions, durations, and legal implications of each state, including what actions are permissible or prohibited during these periods.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Flexibility in Islamic law for those with injuries requiring medical attention
  • Detailed categorization of different types of bleeding and their rulings
  • The importance of understanding minimum and maximum durations for each condition
  • Distinction between religious obligations that are suspended versus those that must be made up
  • The concept of ma'udur (excused person) in Islamic jurisprudence

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Wudu Ablution/ritual purification Hayd Menstruation Nifas Postnatal bleeding Istihada Dysfunctional uterine bleeding Tahr State of purity (opposite of menstruation) Ghusl Major ritual bath Tayammum Dry ablution Tahrima Opening takbir (Allahu Akbar) Ma'udur Person with chronic excuse Ada Habit/regular pattern Khuf Leather socks Janabah State of major ritual impurity I'tikaf Spiritual retreat in mosque Venesection/Phlebotomy Blood drawing from vein

docs.title: Wiping Over Casts
docs.text:
• Permissible to wipe over cast, bandage, or dressing for as long as the excuse remains
  • Hierarchy of washing: 1) Wash injured limb if possible, 2) Wipe limb directly if washing harmful, 3) Wipe over cast/bandage, 4) Leave entirely if wiping causes harm
  • No purity condition required: Unlike khuf, person can be in state of major or minor impurity when applying cast
  • Majority wiping suffices: Not necessary to wipe entire cast, wiping most of it is adequate
  • Cast removal: If cast falls off before healing, previous prayers remain valid

docs.title: Menstruation (Hayd)
docs.text:
• Definition: Blood from uterus of non-pregnant, disease-free woman after adolescence
  • Duration: Minimum 3 days (72 hours), Maximum 10 days (240 hours), Average 5 days
  • Intermittent bleeding: Breaks within 10-day period still count as menstruation
  • Six prohibitions: 1) Intercourse, 2) Tawaf, 3) Entering masjid, 4) Touching between navel and knee without barrier, 5) Reciting Quran, 6) Touching Quran without non-attached barrier

docs.title: Postnatal Bleeding (Nifas)
docs.text:
• Definition: Bleeding after delivery or after most of baby has exited, including after miscarriage with fetal development
  • Duration: No minimum, Maximum 40 days (960 hours)
  • Same rulings as menstruation apply during nifas period

docs.title: Dysfunctional Uterine Bleeding (Istihada)
docs.text:
• Cases: 1) Pregnant woman's bleeding, 2) Bleeding after 40 days postpartum, 3) Bleeding past normal habit exceeding maximum duration, 4) Pre-adolescent girl's bleeding
  • Ruling: Like chronic nosebleed - does not prevent prayer, fasting, intercourse, or tawaf
  • Wudu requirement: New wudu for each prayer time

docs.title: Touching Quran Barriers
docs.text:
• Attached barriers (impermissible): Gloves, sleeves, book covers attached to Quran
  • Non-attached barriers (permissible): Separate cloth, hijab not being worn, detached book cover

docs.title: Masjid Entry During Menstruation
docs.text:
• Hanafi position: Complete prohibition from entering designated masjid boundaries
  • Exception: Areas not designated as masjid (lobbies, non-prayer rooms, rented halls/gymnasiums)
  • Maliki difference: Some permit entry with restrictions (sitting on chairs, not prayer rugs)

docs.title: Habit Establishment and Changes
docs.text:
• Fluctuating cycles: Look to previous month's pattern
  • Exceeding habit but under 10 days: All days count as menstruation
  • Exceeding maximum (10 days): Days beyond habit until day 10 are istihada, requiring makeup prayers

docs.title: Differences Between Schools
docs.text:
• Hanafi: Gloves considered attached barrier for Quran touching
  • Maliki: May permit gloves for Quran handling in teaching contexts
  • Hanafi: Strict masjid entry prohibition during menstruation
  • Maliki: More lenient masjid entry with conditions

docs.title: Wiping Over Medical Devices
docs.text:
• Majority opinion: Wiping most of cast/bandage suffices
  • Minority opinion: Must wipe entire cast (considered weaker)

docs.title: For Those with Medical Conditions
docs.text:
• Consult qualified Muslim doctors about wound exposure to water
  • Follow staged approach: wash if possible → wipe directly → wipe over covering → leave entirely
  • Cast falling off doesn't invalidate previous prayers or current prayer

docs.title: For Women's Worship
docs.text:
• Permitted during menstruation/nifas: Du'as in Arabic, dhikr, listening to Quran, reading hadith (skipping Quran portions), attending non-masjid religious programs
  • Makeup requirements: Fasts must be made up, prayers are not made up
  • Intimacy guidelines: Prohibited during menstruation/nifas, permitted during istihada

docs.title: Habit Tracking and Recognition
docs.text:
• Establish patterns month by month, using previous cycle as reference
  • Any bleeding under maximum duration counts as menstruation if within 10-day limit
  • Bleeding beyond personal habit but under 10 days still counts as menstruation
  • Medical consultation may be needed for unusual bleeding patterns

docs.title: Community Considerations
docs.text:
• Different schools may have varying practices in mixed communities
  • Rented prayer spaces (musallas) have different rules than designated masjids
  • Teaching contexts may have practical accommodations within scholarly boundaries

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session focused on chronic excuses (ma'adur) and the categorization of ritual impurities (najasa). The class began with a review of menstruation (hayd), postnatal bleeding (nifas), and dysfunctional uterine bleeding (istihada), then transitioned to detailed rulings for individuals with chronic excuses. The second half covered the classification of filth into heavy (mugallaza/ghalidha) and light (mukhafafa/khafifa) categories, along with purification methods and permissible amounts for prayer.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Chronic excuses and their three fundamental conditions
  • Equivalence between istihada and other chronic conditions
  • Classification system for ritual impurities
  • Practical purification methods for different types of filth
  • Prayer validity with minimal amounts of impurity
  • Special circumstances requiring accommodation (labor, incontinence, etc.)

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Ma'adur Chronically excused person Istihada Dysfunctional uterine bleeding Najasa Ritual impurity/filth Mugallaza/Ghalidha Heavy filth Mukhafafa/Khafifa Light filth Hayd Menstruation Nifas Postnatal bleeding Tahar State of ritual purity Dhabah Islamic slaughtering method Hummer Wine Mani Sperm/sexual fluid Dirham Coin size (measurement for permissible filth)

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Chronic Excuse Rulings: - Istihada is equivalent to continuous nosebleed in legal ruling - Does not prevent fasting, praying, intercourse, or tawaf - Person makes wudu at beginning of each prayer time - Wudu remains valid despite continuation of the excuse - Wudu breaks only when new prayer time enters or different impurity occurs

Heavy Filth (Najasa Ghalidha) includes: - Spilled/flowing blood from all animals and humans - Feces from land animals - Urine of animals whose meat is impermissible to eat - Saliva of predatory animals and dogs - Wine (hummer) - Sperm/sexual fluid (mani)

Light Filth (Najasa Khafifa) includes: - Urine of animals whose meat is permissible to eat - Urine of horses - Droppings of birds whose meat is not permissible to eat

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Three Conditions for Chronic Excuses:

  1. Establishment of Excuse: Must continue for entire prayer time, preventing person from making wudu and praying without recurrence

  2. Continuity: Must occur at least once in every subsequent prayer time, even if intermittent

  3. Termination: Entire prayer time passes without the excuse occurring

Blood Classification Exemptions: - Blood remaining in meat and vessels of properly slaughtered animals - Blood in liver, spleen, and heart - Blood that surfaces but doesn't flow - Blood of insects, mosquitoes, roaches - Fish blood

Purification Methods: - Discernible filth: Remove the physical body of filth - Indiscernible filth: Wash and squeeze three times, or wash three times with waiting periods

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text: Not covered in this session.

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Permissible Amounts for Prayer: - Heavy filth: Size of dirham (inner palm area when water is poured) - Light filth: Less than one-fourth of garment or body - Light spray of urine: Dots no larger than pinhead are excused

Special Circumstances: - Women in labor can receive assistance with wudu - Wet towels acceptable for wudu if water drips off body - Wudu socks recommended for public situations - Chronic excuse holders should wait until end of prayer time to establish excuse

Practical Guidelines: - Person with chronic excuse makes fresh wudu for each prayer time - Can pray multiple obligatory and voluntary prayers within same prayer time - Different impurity (other than established excuse) breaks wudu immediately - Difficult-to-remove filth requires reasonable effort with cleaning agents

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered detailed rulings on najasa (impurity) transference between clean and dirty materials, conditions for purification of various substances, and extensive discussion on Islamic hunting and slaughtering methods. Key focus on when filth transfers between damp/dry materials and specific purification methods for different objects.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Transference of najasa between clean and dirty materials
  • Conditions under which impurity does NOT transfer
  • Purification methods for different materials (leather, metal, earth, body)
  • Islamic hunting regulations and permissible methods
  • Machine slaughtering vs. hand slaughtering debate
  • Distinction between piercing and bludgeoning weapons in hunting

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Najasa Impurity, ritual uncleanliness Tahir Pure, ritually clean Mustamad Used water (water that has been used for purification) Istihala Complete transformation/chemical change Khamer Wine, alcoholic beverage Ma'dhur Excused person (due to continuous discharge) Tasmiyyah Reciting "Bismillah" before slaughter/hunt Amal al-Kathir Excessive movement (in prayer) Maal Kathir Large quantity of water Maal Qaleel Small quantity of water

docs.title: Najasa Transference Rules
docs.text:
• Light spray of urine: Excused if each dot is no larger than pin head size (unavoidable amount)
  • Moisture from damp impure garment: If wrung out and no water drips, the originally clean cloth remains pure
  • Wet clean foot on impure ground: Remains pure if no visible trace of filth appears
  • Damp pure garment on impure ground: Remains pure if no trace of impurity visible

docs.title: Purification Methods
docs.text:
• Water and cleaning agents: Any liquid that removes filth (rose water, vinegar, modern detergents)
  • Istihala (transformation): Complete chemical change purifies (wine → vinegar, feces → salt/ashes)
  • Non-absorbent materials: Leather socks, boots, sandals purified by scraping/rubbing if filth has solid body
  • Metal objects: Swords, knives purified by wiping surface
  • Natural earth: Purified by drying if no visible/smell traces remain

docs.title: Animal Parts and Death
docs.text:
• Non-flowing blood parts remain pure: Hair, fur, cut feathers, horns, claws, bones (without fat)
  • Plucked feathers: Considered impure (unlike cut feathers)
  • Animal hides: Purified through proper tanning or Islamic slaughter
  • Exception: Swine and human skin cannot be purified even by tanning

docs.title: Hunting Regulations
docs.text:
• Required conditions: Recite Bismillah, use sharp piercing object, wound must cause death
  • Sharp bullets/arrows: Permissible if they pierce rather than bludgeon
  • Bludgeoning weapons: Impermissible unless animal is wounded then immediately slaughtered
  • Machine slaughter: Generally impermissible due to inability to recite Tasmiyyah for each animal

docs.title: Najasa Transference Conditions
docs.text:
1. Damp impure cloth + dry pure cloth: Pure cloth remains clean if no dripping when wrung
  2. Only urine dampness: Transfers impurity regardless of dripping test
  3. Water mixed with urine: Follows dripping test rule

docs.title: Hunting Weapon Categories
docs.text:
1. Piercing weapons (halal): Sharp arrows, pointed bullets, spears
  2. Bludgeoning weapons (haram): Blunt bullets, pellets, hammers
  3. Combination approach: Wound with bludgeoning weapon then immediate slaughter

docs.title: Slaughter Requirements
docs.text:
• Horizontal cut mandatory: Vertical cuts impermissible (cannot cut 3 of 4 veins)
  • Three of four veins: Must be severed (two jugulars, windpipe, food pipe)
  • Sharp blade required: Smooth blade preferred over serrated
  • Tasmiyyah: Must be recited before each slaughter

docs.title: Machine Slaughter Debate
docs.text:
• Hanafi position: Generally impermissible due to inability to recite individual Tasmiyyah
  • Shafi'i opinion mentioned: Some allow Bismillah recited over machine/knife for day's slaughter
  • Contemporary challenge: Speed of machines makes individual Tasmiyyah impossible

docs.title: Stunning Animals
docs.text:
• Electrical stunning: Discouraged due to causing discomfort
  • Levels of discomfort: Some discomfort unavoidable, but excessive stunning problematic
  • Practical consideration: Balancing animal welfare with slaughter requirements

docs.title: Modern Challenges
docs.text:
• Corporate washrooms: Small water splashes from flushing generally excused
  • Leather products: Verify Islamic slaughter and avoid swine/mixed materials
  • Hunting sports: Understand weapon types and proper Islamic procedures
  • Food industry: Be cautious of machine-slaughtered meat labeling

docs.title: Practical Guidelines
docs.text:
• Blood stains: Washing effort required; remaining stains after washing are excusable
  • Children's clothing: Infant urine ruling may differ based on diet (milk vs. solid food)
  • Workplace prayer: Minor unavoidable impurities don't prevent prayer
  • Hunting trips: Always recite Bismillah; distinguish between piercing and bludgeoning weapons

docs.title: Contemporary Applications
docs.text:
• Halal certification: Requires verification of slaughter method, not just Muslim slaughterer
  • Leather goods: Modern tanning may not purify non-halal animal hides
  • Hunting licenses: Islamic hunting rules apply alongside legal requirements
  • Food preparation: Understanding transference rules for kitchen hygiene and prayer validity

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This class continued the purification chapter, focusing on wells and water sources that may become impure, saliva categories affecting water purity, and concluded with the chapter on cleansing after relieving oneself (istinja). The session covered detailed rulings on when wells become impure based on what falls into them, how to purify wells based on animal sizes, and four categories of animal saliva affecting water purity. The class concluded with istibra (for males) and istinja procedures.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Wells as water sources for purification and their contamination rules
  • Size-based categorization for well purification procedures
  • Saliva categories determining water usability for wudu and drinking
  • Proper etiquette and procedures for istinja (post-toilet cleansing)
  • Distinction between istibra (males) and general cleansing requirements
  • Practical applications of purity laws in daily life

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Najasa Impurity, filth Istinja Cleansing after relieving oneself Istibra Removing remaining urine from male private parts (obligatory for men) Makruh Tahrimi Strongly disliked, bordering on forbidden Makruh Tanzihi Mildly disliked Janabah State of major ritual impurity Qibla Direction of prayer (toward Mecca) Wajib Obligatory Mustahab Recommended Yaqeen Certainty

docs.title: Small Wells
docs.text:
• Ruling: Small wells become impure if any filth falls in, even without visible traces
  • Examples: Urine, blood, chicken droppings
  • Exception: Very small amounts of animal dung that observers don't consider "large"

docs.title: Things That DO NOT Make Wells Impure
docs.text:
1. Pigeon or sparrow droppings
  2. Dead creatures without flowing blood (large mosquitoes, flies, wasps, scorpions)
  3. Dead sea creatures (born and residing in water - fish, crabs, frogs)
  4. Living creatures that fall in and come out alive (if no filth on body)

docs.title: Purification by Bucket Removal
docs.text:
Based on animal size that died in well:

  1. Small animals (mouse-sized): Remove 20 buckets
  2. Medium animals (pigeon-sized): Remove 40 buckets
  3. Large animals: Remove ALL water
  4. Any bloated/fragmented animal (regardless of size): Remove ALL water
  5. If removing all water is difficult: Remove 200 buckets

Note: Bucket size = average/customary size recognized in that locality

docs.title: 1. Pure and Purifying (Not Disliked)
docs.text:
• Human saliva (clean mouth, even if in janabah or non-Muslim)
  • Horses
  • Animals whose meat is halal to eat

docs.title: 2. Impure (Cannot Use)
docs.text:
• Dogs, pigs
  • Predatory land animals (lions, wolves, hyenas, apes)
  • Exception: Can drink only in dire need (life-threatening thirst)

docs.title: 3. Disliked (Makruh Tanzihi if alternatives available)
docs.text:
• House cats
  • Released chickens (not enclosed, roaming freely)
  • Predatory birds (hawks, falcons)
  • Creatures roaming houses (mice, snakes)

docs.title: 4. Doubtful Purifying Nature
docs.text:
• Mules and donkeys
  • Ruling: If no other water available, make wudu then perform tayammum

docs.title: Chickens Distinction
docs.text:
• Released chickens: Disliked (roam and may consume filth)
  • Enclosed chickens: Not disliked (fed pure food, avoid filth except their own)

docs.title: Well Water vs. Large Bodies of Water
docs.text:
• Small wells follow bucket removal rules
  • Large bodies of water: Can use different area if contamination is localized
  • Flowing water: Different area usage generally permissible

docs.title: Istibra (Males Only)
docs.text:
• Obligation: Must ensure no urine drops remain in urethra
  • Methods: Walking, clearing throat, lying on side, gentle squeezing
  • Purpose: Prevent wudu invalidation from subsequent urine drops
  • When: Must be done before istinja

docs.title: Istinja Guidelines
docs.text:
• Sunnah to clean after using restroom
  • Not required after passing gas (gas is not impure)
  • Procedure: Use toilet paper first, then wash with water
  • Direction: Wipe forward and backwards until clean
  • Goal: Remove bad smell and convince oneself area is clean

docs.title: Restroom Etiquette
docs.text:
• Enter: Left foot first, seek Allah's protection
  • During: Don't face qibla, sun, or moon; avoid unnecessary speech
  • Exit: Right foot first, say "Alhamdulillahi alladhi adhaba anni al-adha..."
  • Makruh Tahrimi: Using right hand, bones, dry dung, food, valuable items (silk/cotton)

docs.title: Practical Notes
docs.text:
• Next class begins with comprehensive test on entire purification chapter
  • Principle: When in doubt about purity but no original certainty existed, err on side of caution
  • Custom and locality determine "average bucket size" for well purification

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session introduced the chapter on Salah (ritual prayer), beginning with an explanation of why purification (Tahara) is studied before prayer in Islamic jurisprudence. The class covered the five daily prayer times, their scriptural basis, and scholarly differences regarding specific timing boundaries. Key focus was placed on the differences of opinion between Imam Abu Hanifa and other scholars regarding Dhuhr/Asr timing boundaries and Maghrib/Isha transitions.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Prerequisite relationship between purification and prayer: Tahara must precede Salah
  • Divine establishment of prayer times: Allah has ordained specific times for each prayer
  • Scholarly methodology in determining prayer boundaries: Using shadow measurements and celestial observations
  • Jurisprudential differences: Various valid opinions among classical scholars on timing boundaries
  • Practical application: Following local mosque practices while understanding different madhab positions

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Salah/Salawat Ritual prayer(s) Tahara Purification Fajr Subah Dawn prayer Sadiq True dawn (horizontal white light) Kadhib False dawn (vertical light) Zawal Movement away from midday position Istiwa When sun is directly overhead Dhuhr Midday prayer Asr Afternoon prayer Maghrib Sunset prayer Isha Night prayer Witr Odd-numbered prayer after Isha Mithl Awwal First shadow (excluding original) Mithl Thani Second shadow (excluding original) Shafaq Ahmar Red twilight Shafaq Abyad White twilight Imamah of Jibreel Jibreel leading the Prophet in prayer

docs.title: Fiqh Rulings & Evidence
docs.text:
Primary Evidence for Prayer Obligation: - Quran: "Indeed, prayer has been decreed upon the believers a decree of specified times" (Surah An-Nisa, 4:103) - Hadith of Isra wal Mi'raj: Originally 50 prayers reduced to 5 with reward of 50 - Hadith of Jibreel's Imamah: Angel Jibreel taught prayer times by leading Prophet for two days

Prayer Time Determinations: 1. Fajr: From true dawn (Sadiq) until right before sunrise 2. Dhuhr: From after midday (Zawal) until shadow length varies by opinion 3. Asr: Begins when Dhuhr ends, continues until right before sunset 4. Maghrib: From sunset until twilight disappears (red or white - scholarly difference) 5. Isha: From end of Maghrib time until right before Fajr

docs.title: Conditions & Categories
docs.text:
Shadow Measurement Method for Midday: - Place stick vertically in even ground - Observe shadow length - When shadow stops shortening = midday has passed - Dhuhr time begins immediately after

Categories of Dawn: - False Dawn (Kadhib): Vertical light extending upward - True Dawn (Sadiq): Horizontal white light across horizon - Time difference: Approximately 12 minutes (3 degrees of sun's angle)

Twilight Categories: - White Twilight: Appears first after sunset - Red Twilight: Appears after white twilight disappears

docs.title: Scholarly Opinions
docs.text:
Dhuhr Ending Time (When Asr Begins): - Imam Abu Hanifa: Shadow equals twice object's length (excluding original shadow) - Results in longer Dhuhr time - Imam Abu Yusuf, Imam Muhammad, Imam Shafi'i: Shadow equals object's length (excluding original) - Results in earlier Asr time - Imam Tahawi: Preferred the second opinion (companions' view)

Maghrib Ending Time: - Imam Abu Hanifa: Ends when white twilight disappears (shorter Maghrib time) - Imam Abu Yusuf & Imam Muhammad: Ends when red twilight disappears (longer Maghrib time)

Practical Application: - Followers may pray according to local mosque timing - No contradiction when following valid scholarly opinions - Different madhabs represent legitimate interpretations

docs.title: Lessons & Practical Application
docs.text:
Spiritual Reflection: - Human weakness acknowledged: "And mankind was created weak" (Quran) - Contrast with pet owners' dedication: Non-Muslims wake early for pets while Muslims struggle with Fajr - Gratitude principle: Remember Allah's countless blessings when feeling lazy about prayer

Practical Guidelines: - When traveling or with excuse: Some flexibility in following different timing opinions - As residents: Generally follow your chosen madhab's timing - Local mosque attendance: Acceptable to follow mosque's established prayer times - Witr prayer: Must be prayed after Isha, not before (sequential order required)

Contemporary Challenges: - Modern tendency to prioritize worldly matters over prayer obligations - Weekly vs. daily mosque attendance disparity (Jumu'ah vs. daily prayers) - Need for consistent commitment despite seasonal challenges (cold weather, comfortable bedding)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered prayer timing details, recommended prayer times (mustahab), forbidden prayer times, combining prayers, and the fundamentals of Adhan and Iqamah. The class addressed practical questions about shadows in determining Asr time, differences between Hanafi and other madhabs regarding prayer timing and combination, and proper etiquette for calling Adhan.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Differences in shadow calculations between Imam Abu Hanifa and his students for Asr timing
  • Recommended times for prayers based on seasons and weather conditions
  • Forbidden times when no prayers can be performed
  • Rules for combining prayers in Hanafi madhab vs other schools
  • Proper performance of Adhan and Iqamah
  • Practical applications for following different madhabs in community settings

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Isfar When slight/dim light appears in the sky (later than ghalas) Ghalas Absolute darkness, early dawn time Subh Sadiq True dawn Makroo Tahrimi Prohibitively disliked (sinful) Qada Makeup prayers Jamaa Haqiqi Actual combination of prayers in one time Jamaa Suri Apparent combination (praying one at end of time, next at beginning) Wasila Station of mediation (for Prophet ﷺ) Muezzin/Mu'adhdhin One who calls the Adhan

docs.title: Prayer Timing Differences:
docs.text:
• Asr timing according to Abu Hanifa: When shadow = 2 times original + object's shadow
  • Asr timing according to Abu Yusuf & Muhammad: When shadow = 1 time original + object's shadow
  • Example: 6-foot person → Abu Yusuf/Muhammad: 12 feet total shadow; Abu Hanifa: 18 feet total shadow

docs.title: Forbidden Prayer Times:
docs.text: Three times when ALL prayers (fard, qada, sajdatul tilawah) are INVALID: 1. At sunrise 2. At midday (zawal) 3. At sunset - Exception: Only Asr of that specific day can be prayed at sunset

docs.title: Makroo Times for Voluntary Prayers:
docs.text:
1. The three forbidden times above
  2. During Arabic khutbah
  3. Before Fajr fard (except 2 rakats sunnah)
  4. After Fajr fard until after sunrise
  5. After Asr fard until sunset

docs.title: Recommended Prayer Times (Mustahab):
docs.text: Fajr: During isfar (when light begins to appear) Zuhr: - Summer: Delay when weather is hot for cooling - Winter: Pray early after zawal Asr: Delay until before sun changes (can look at sun without harm) Maghrib: Immediately upon sunset Isha: In cloudy weather, pray immediately; otherwise delay somewhat Cloudy Weather Rule: Pray Asr and Isha immediately; delay other prayers

docs.title: Adhan Requirements:
docs.text:
• Called in Arabic only
  • Caller should be righteous, know proper timing and method
  • Should be in state of ritual purity
  • Face qibla, fingers in ears
  • Turn face right for "Hayya 'ala salah", left for "Hayya 'ala falah"

docs.title: Prayer Combination Differences:
docs.text:
Hanafi Position: - Only jamaa suri allowed (except Hajj) - Pray Zuhr at very end of time, then Asr immediately when time begins - Actual combination (jamaa haqiqi) only during Hajj at Arafah and Muzdalifah

Other Madhabs: Allow actual combination during travel

docs.title: Ibadat vs Muamalat:
docs.text:
• Ibadat (worship matters): Follow Imam Abu Hanifa's opinion
  • Muamalat (worldly dealings): Follow opinions of Abu Yusuf and Muhammad
  • Reasoning: Students' opinions were often Abu Hanifa's earlier positions before he revised them

docs.title: Witr Prayer Methods:
docs.text:
• Hanafi way: 3 rakats with tashahhud in 2nd, dua qunoot before rukoo in 3rd
  • Other methods: 2+1 separate, or 3 without middle tashahhud
  • All four imams agree on 3 rakats total, differ in methodology

docs.title: Community Prayer Situations:
docs.text:
• When praying with family/community following different madhab, respectfully follow your own school
  • For witr differences in community: Ask local mufti for guidance, may pray separately at home
  • When pressed for time in school/work: Can follow local timing if ensures prayer completion

docs.title: Adhan Mistakes to Avoid:
docs.text:
• Falah (correct) vs Falah (barren land - incorrect)
  • Allahu Akbar (correct) vs Allah Akbar (changes meaning - incorrect)
  • Proper Arabic pronunciation essential for validity

docs.title: Travel and Combination:
docs.text:
• Hanafi traveler should use jamaa suri method when needed
  • At home: Call Adhan and Iqamah even for individual prayer
  • When combining: One Adhan, two Iqamahs for Zuhr-Asr

docs.title: Practical Timing Issues:
docs.text:
• True dawn difficult to see in urban areas due to light pollution
  • Can rely on calculated prayer times when unable to observe natural signs
  • Fajr ends at actual sunrise (sun's disk visible), not just sky illumination

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This class covered the conditions (shara'it) and integrals (arkan) of prayer, establishing the foundation for valid salah. The instructor explained the differences between conditions that must be met before prayer begins and integrals that are part of the prayer itself. Key discussions included prayer requirements, physical purity, facing the qibla, and the mandatory requisites (wajibat) that complete the prayer structure.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Conditions vs. Integrals: Clear distinction between pre-prayer requirements and in-prayer obligations
  • Physical and Spiritual Preparation: Emphasis on ritual purity, cleanliness, and proper covering
  • Prayer Structure: Understanding the bare minimum requirements versus complete prayer format
  • Flexibility in Exceptional Circumstances: Allowances when normal conditions cannot be met
  • Gender Considerations: Different prayer requirements and modesty considerations for men and women

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Shara'it Conditions (of prayer) Arkan Integrals/pillars (of prayer) Wajibat Mandatory requisites Wudu Minor ritual ablution Ghusl Major ritual ablution Janabah State of major ritual impurity Aura Parts of body that must be covered Qibla Direction of prayer (toward Kaaba) Jihah Direction Tahrima Opening takbir (Allahu Akbar) Takbiratul tahrima The opening takbir that begins prayer Qiyam Standing in prayer Ruku Bowing Sujud/Sajdah Prostration Mawadi'u sujud Places upon which prostration is performed Qa'adatul akhira Final sitting Tashahud Testimony recited while sitting Ta'adil al-arkan Performing prayer parts with proper composure Qaadatul ula First sitting (in 3+ rakah prayers) Qunoot Special supplication in Witr prayer Taslim/Salam Closing salutation of prayer Sirran Silent recitation Jahran Loud recitation

docs.title: Conditions (Shara'it) of Prayer
docs.text:
Seven essential conditions must be met before prayer begins:

  1. Ritual Purity: Must be in state of wudu; if in janabah, must perform ghusl
  2. Physical Cleanliness: Person, clothing, and prayer place must be free from filth (except excused amounts)
  3. Covering Nakedness (Aura):
  4. Men: From below navel to below knees (knees included)
  5. Women: Entire body except face, hands, and feet
  6. Slave women: Same as men except abdomen and back must also be covered
  7. Facing Qibla:
  8. In Makkah: Face Kaaba directly
  9. Elsewhere: Face direction of Qibla (±45 degrees acceptable due to distance)
  10. Prayer Time: Must be within designated time for each prayer
  11. Intention: Must know which prayer is being performed (place: heart)
  12. Tahrima: Opening takbir "Allahu Akbar" must be uttered aloud (place: tongue)

docs.title: Integrals (Arkan) of Prayer
docs.text:
Six essential components that if missed invalidate the prayer:

  1. Tahrima: Opening takbir (according to Imam Muhammad; others consider it a condition)
  2. Standing (Qiyam): If physically able (not required for voluntary prayers)
  3. Quranic Recitation: Minimum one verse in any two rakahs of obligatory prayer, all rakahs of witr/voluntary
  4. Bowing (Ruku)
  5. Prostration (Sujud): Must touch ground with forehead, both hands, both knees, bottom of toes
  6. Final Sitting: For duration of tashahud recitation

docs.title: Key Principle
docs.text: Prayer must be ended by one's own action, and it is wajib that this action be saying "as-salam"

docs.title: Exceptional Circumstances - Conditions May Be Overlooked:
docs.text:
• Lack of water for purification when needed for survival
  • Insufficient clothing to cover aura
  • Inability to face qibla due to captivity, sickness, or safety concerns
  • Unknown qibla direction (make educated guess using sun, stars, etc.)

docs.title: Prayer Categories by Recitation:
docs.text:
• Silent Prayers (Sirran): Zuhr, Asr
  • Loud Prayers (Jahran): Fajr, Maghrib, Isha (first two rakahs only)

docs.title: Recitation Rules:
docs.text:
• Imam: Recites aloud in designated prayers, silently in others
  • Followers: No recitation required (imam's recitation suffices for all)
  • Individual Prayer: Must recite in all rakahs

docs.title: Tahrima Classification:
docs.text:
• Imam Muhammad: Considers tahrima an integral (rukn) of prayer
  • Imam Abu Hanifa & Abu Yusuf: Consider it a condition (shart)
  • Practical Effect: Same in both opinions - cannot be omitted

docs.title: Bismillah in Prayer:
docs.text:
• Difference of Opinion: Whether "Bismillahir Rahmanir Rahim" is a verse of Quran or separator between surahs
  • Practice: Some recite it aloud, others silently, based on this difference

docs.title: Prayer Preparation:
docs.text:
• Ensure ritual and physical purity before beginning
  • Use available technology (apps, compass) to find qibla direction
  • Dress appropriately to cover aura requirements

docs.title: During Prayer:
docs.text:
• Practice ta'adil al-arkan: Don't rush through prayer components
  • Maintain proper posture and stillness in each position
  • Follow imam closely without getting ahead

docs.title: Special Considerations:
docs.text:
• Women's Prayer: More covered positions to maintain modesty
  • Travel Situations: Make educated guesses when exact conditions unavailable
  • Following Imam: Trust that imam's recitation covers followers' obligation

docs.title: Completing Prayer:
docs.text:
• Must end with salam ("As-salamu alaykum wa rahmatullah")
  • Turn right first, then left
  • Don't get ahead of imam when following in congregation

docs.title: Mandatory Requisites Include:
docs.text:
• Specific "Allahu Akbar" for tahrima
  • Surah Fatiha plus additional verses/surah in first two rakahs
  • Proper prostration with forehead and nose
  • Complete tashahud in both sittings
  • Six extra takbirs in Eid prayers
  • Qunoot recitation in Witr prayer

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered the mandatory requisites (wajibat) and recommended acts (sunnah) of prayer, followed by the etiquette (adab/mustahabbat) of prayer. The class then discussed various supplications recited during prayer and concluded with actions that invalidate the prayer. The instructor emphasized the difference between fard (obligatory), wajib (mandatory requisites), and sunnah (recommended) acts, using the analogy of a house structure versus its beautification.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Classification of prayer acts: Fard vs Wajib vs Sunnah
  • The beautification concept: Sunnah acts enhance the basic structure of prayer
  • Proper gaze direction throughout different prayer positions
  • Hanafi madhab rulings on follower's silence during congregational prayer
  • Actions and behaviors that invalidate prayer
  • Flexibility in making up missed components through sajdat sahw

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Wajibat Mandatory requisites of prayer Sunnah Recommended acts Adab/Mustahabbat Etiquette/preferred acts Tahrima Opening takbir of prayer Thana Opening supplication Ta'awud Seeking refuge (A'udhu billahi min ash-shaytani'r-rajim) Tasmiyyah Bismillahi'r-rahmani'r-rahim Takbirat al-intiqaliyya Takbirs of movement Qira'ah Recitation Durood Ibrahimi Blessings upon Prophet Ibrahim Tahiyyat Tashahhud/sitting recitation Qunut Special supplication in Witr Sajdat Sahw Prostration of forgetfulness

docs.title: Sunnah Acts of Prayer (16 total):
docs.text:
1. Raising hands during tahrima: Men raise to ears, women to shoulders
  2. Hand placement: Men place right over left under navel, women on chest under breasts
  3. Reciting thana (opening supplication)
  4. Reciting ta'awud in first rakah only
  5. Reciting basmala before Fatiha in every rakah (except followers remain silent)
  6. Saying Ameen after Fatiha and "Rabbana lakal hamd"
  7. Silent recitation of ta'awud, basmala, ameen, tahmeed
  8. Imam's audible takbir
  9. Takbirat al-intiqaliyya (movement takbirs)
  10. Tasbih three times in ruku and sujud
  11. Reciting Fatiha in 3rd-4th rakah of four-rakah prayer and 3rd of Maghrib
  12. Durood Ibrahimi in final sitting
  13. Final du'a using words that resemble divine speech
  14. Turning head right and left during salam
  15. Intention for greetings when making salam
  16. Various other recommended acts

docs.title: Wajib vs Fard Distinction:
docs.text:
• Qira'ah (recitation) is fard in any two rakahs of obligatory prayer
  • Reciting Fatiha specifically is wajib in first two rakahs
  • Reciting surah after Fatiha is wajib in first two rakahs
  • Missing wajib acts can be remedied with sajdat sahw

docs.title: Follower's Rules in Congregation:
docs.text:
• Remains silent during recitation (Hanafi position)
  • Recites thana, ta'awud, tahiyyat, and du'as silently
  • Does not recite Fatiha or surahs when following imam
  • Makes intention for imam's position when making salam

docs.title: Prayer Invalidators (16 categories):
docs.text:
1. Human speech - even if forgetful
  2. Excessive movement - if observer cannot tell person is praying
  3. Eating - any external amount; internal residue up to chickpea size permitted
  4. Drinking - any amount
  5. Throat clearing with intentional letter utterance
  6. Crying/moaning from pain (not from remembrance of Allah)
  7. Greeting others with intention of responding
  8. Handshaking
  9. Responding to statements with dhikr
  10. Turning torso away from qibla
  11. Du'as resembling normal conversation
  12. Elongating hamza in takbir (saying "Allahu" instead of "Allahu")
  13. Uncovering nakedness (1/4 of limb for duration of one integral)
  14. Carrying unexcused filth
  15. Follower preceding imam without makeup
  16. Missing prayer integral without later makeup

docs.title: Hanafi Madhab Positions:
docs.text:
• Follower's silence: Complete silence during imam's recitation
  • Basmala: Recited silently before Fatiha in every rakah
  • Ameen: Said silently after Fatiha
  • Takbir: Imam says audibly, followers silently

docs.title: Areas of Scholarly Difference:
docs.text:
• Whether followers should recite Fatiha behind imam
  • Audible vs silent recitation of various elements
  • Specific wording and practices of prayer components

docs.title: Key Practical Points:
docs.text:
1. House analogy: Fard acts are the structure, sunnah acts beautify the prayer
  2. Gaze direction: Always maintain low gaze throughout prayer positions
  3. Movement guidelines: Avoid excessive movement that makes prayer unclear to observers
  4. Makeup prayers: When late to congregation, complete missed rakahs after imam's salam
  5. Error correction: Many mistakes can be remedied through sajdat sahw rather than invalidating prayer
  6. Intention matters: Actions done intentionally vs accidentally have different rulings

docs.title: Etiquette Guidelines:
docs.text:
• Remove coverings from hands during opening takbir
  • Maintain proper gaze in each position (standing: place of sajda, ruku: feet, sajda: nose tip, sitting: lap, salam: shoulders)
  • Refrain from unnecessary coughing, cover mouth when yawning
  • Focus on meaning of recitations when not actively reciting (for followers)

docs.title: Common Scenarios:
docs.text:
• Late arrival: Join imam's position, complete missed portions after his salam
  • Forgotten components: Use sajdat sahw for missed wajib acts
  • Congregational movement: Limited movement acceptable to close gaps in rows
  • Language considerations: Du'as must avoid resembling casual conversation

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered fundamental aspects of prayer etiquette and rulings, focusing on things disliked (makruhat) and permissible actions during prayer, prayer invalidation, abandoning prayer, prayer while traveling, and the prayer of travelers. The discussion included practical applications for contemporary Muslims regarding prayer conduct, travel distances, and various scenarios encountered in modern life.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Distinction between makruh tahrimi (prohibitively disliked) and makruh tanzihi (mildly disliked) actions in prayer
  • Proper conduct and movement during salah to maintain focus and validity
  • Understanding when prayer becomes invalid vs. merely disliked
  • Travel prayer (qasr) regulations and distance requirements
  • Practical guidance for prayer in vehicles and during journeys
  • Emergency situations that permit breaking prayer

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Fard Obligatory act proven with concrete evidence Wajib Necessary act (specific to Hanafi madhhab, equal to fard in action but different in proof level) Makruh tahrimi Prohibitively disliked Makruh tanzihi Mildly disliked Abath Unnecessary actions/movements Amal kathir Excessive movement that invalidates prayer Sutra Barrier placed in front during prayer Qasr Shortened prayer for travelers Awrah Parts of body that must be covered Nawafil Voluntary prayers Sunnah mu'akkadah Emphasized sunnah prayers

docs.title: Things Disliked in Prayer (Makruhat):
docs.text:
Prohibitively Disliked (Makruh Tahrimi): 1. Deliberately omitting any wajib act (invalidates prayer) 2. Excessive unnecessary movements (fiddling with garments/body) 3. Wiping away pebbles more than once during prayer 4. Cracking knuckles 5. Placing hands on hips 6. Turning neck (turning chest invalidates prayer)

Actions That Make Prayer Invalid: - Deliberately omitting wajib acts - Excessive movement (amal kathir) - Turning chest away from qibla - Uncovering awrah for duration of one complete integral

Distance for Travel Prayer: - Minimum 48 miles or 3 days journey at medium pace - Must intend to travel this distance - Prayer shortening begins when leaving city limits, not at 48-mile mark

docs.title: Prayer Shortening Rules:
docs.text:
• Four-rakah prayers (Dhuhr, Asr, Isha) become two rakahs
  • Fajr remains two rakahs, Maghrib remains three rakahs
  • Duration: Less than 15 full days at destination
  • If staying 15+ days, pray full prayers as resident

docs.title: Emergency Prayer Breaking:
docs.text: Permissible to break prayer for: - Robbery of items worth one dirham or more - Helping someone in emergency - Preventing blind person from falling into danger - Midwife assisting in delivery

docs.title: Prayer in Vehicles:
docs.text:
• Obligatory prayers invalid in moving vehicles except necessity
  • Voluntary prayers permitted with head movements only
  • Must face qibla when stationary

docs.title: Hanafi Madhhab Distinctions:
docs.text:
• Concept of wajib unique to Hanafi school
  • Fard: proven with concrete evidence without doubt
  • Wajib: proven evidence with some level of interpretive possibility
  • Both equal in practical application but different in evidential strength

docs.title: Travel Prayer Rulings:
docs.text:
• Resident following traveler imam: completes full prayer in current prayer time only
  • Traveler following resident imam: must complete full prayer with imam
  • Missed prayers made up according to state when missed (traveler = 2, resident = 4)

docs.title: Modern Applications:
docs.text:
1. Vehicle Prayer: Stop for obligatory prayers when possible; use vehicles only for voluntary prayers while traveling
  2. Clothing Considerations: Avoid images on garments, ensure proper covering, remove distracting hanging elements
  3. Travel Planning: Calculate 48-mile distance for determining travel prayer status
  4. Emergency Situations: Prioritize human safety over prayer completion when necessary

docs.title: Contemporary Guidance:
docs.text:
• Pictures in books/magazines should be covered or turned away during prayer
  • Nail polish and barriers must be removed for valid wudu
  • Concentrate during prayer by closing eyes if needed for focus
  • Use sutra (barrier) when praying in public spaces

docs.title: Practical Tips:
docs.text:
• Keep prayer area free from distracting images or objects
  • Minimize unnecessary movements during prayer
  • Plan travel prayers according to departure from city limits
  • Maintain proper dress code avoiding transparent or image-bearing clothing

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covered various aspects of Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) including Q&A from previous classes, prayer of the sick person (salat al-marid), missed prayers (qada salawat), the concept of Sahibu Tarteeb, emphasized sunnah prayers (sunnah mu'akkadah), night vigil prayers (tarawih), and cultural considerations in Islamic practice. The class emphasized the importance of maintaining prayer obligations regardless of physical condition and the systematic approach to making up missed prayers.

docs.title: Key Themes
docs.text:
• Prayer adaptability based on physical ability and health conditions
  • Systematic approach to making up missed prayers (qada)
  • The concept of Sahibu Tarteeb and its implications
  • Distinction between emphasized and non-emphasized sunnah prayers
  • Proper conduct regarding cultural events and Islamic boundaries
  • The flexibility of Islamic law while maintaining core principles

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Qasr Shortened prayer (for travelers) Qada Making up missed prayers Sahibu Tarteeb Person who has missed less than six salawat and must maintain prayer order Sunnah Mu'akkadah Emphasized sunnah prayers Sunnah Ghayr Mu'akkadah Non-emphasized sunnah prayers Tarawih Night vigil prayers during Ramadan Witr Odd-numbered prayer (3 rakah) Qunut Supplication recited in witr prayer Fidya Expiatory payment for missed prayers Watan al-Asli Primary residence Watan al-Iqamah Temporary residence Tahiyyat al-Masjid Two rakah greeting prayer upon entering mosque Salat al-Duha Late morning prayer Qiyam al-Layl Night vigil prayer

docs.title: Prayer of the Sick Person
docs.text:
1. Standing difficulty: Pray sitting with normal bowing and prostration
  2. Prostration difficulty: Use head movements, making prostration lower than bowing
  3. Sitting impossible: Pray lying down (back or side) facing qibla with head movements
  4. No head movement possible: Absolved from prayer during that state
  5. Duration rule: If unable to move head for 24+ hours, most scholars say no makeup required
  6. Consciousness rule: If unconscious for more than 5 prayers, no makeup required; less than 5 prayers must be made up

docs.title: Fidya (Expiatory Payment)
docs.text:
• Amount: Half sa'a (2.2 kg) of wheat or one full sa'a (4.4 kg) of barley, or cash equivalent
  • Source: Taken from one-third of deceased's estate
  • Recipients: Must be given to indigent (fuqara)
  • Method: Cannot pray on behalf of deceased; only monetary compensation allowed

docs.title: Sahibu Tarteeb Rules
docs.text:
• Definition: Person who has missed less than six salawat
  • Order requirement: Must make up missed prayers before current ones
  • Four or less rule: If praying 4 or fewer salawat after missed prayer, they become invalid when makeup is done
  • Five or more rule: If praying 5+ salawat after missed prayer, previous prayers remain valid
  • Exceptions: Time constraint or forgetfulness nullify the order requirement

docs.title: Witr Prayer Conditions
docs.text:
• Mandatory (Wajib): 3 rakah with one salam
  • Qunut placement: Before ruku in third rakah
  • Congregation: Permissible only in Ramadan for groups
  • Makeup rule: If joining imam in 3rd rakah (even after qunut), no qunut needed in makeup

docs.title: Emphasized Sunnah (Sunnah Mu'akkadah)
docs.text:
Two Rakah Prayers: - Before Fajr - After Zuhr (2 rakah) - After Maghrib (2 rakah) - After Isha before Witr (2 rakah)

Four Rakah Prayers: - Before Zuhr (4 rakah with one salam) - Before and after Jummah

docs.title: Non-Emphasized Sunnah
docs.text:
• 4 rakah before Asr
  • 4 rakah before Isha
  • 4 rakah after Isha
  • 6 rakah after Maghrib (with 3 salams)
  • Tahiyyat al-Masjid (2 rakah)
  • After Wudu (2 rakah)
  • Salat al-Duha (4-12 rakah)
  • Istikharah prayer
  • Night prayers during last 10 nights of Ramadan
  • Night prayers during first 10 nights of Dhul-Hijjah

docs.title: Tarawih Prayer
docs.text:
• Hanafi position: 20 rakah in sets of 2 with 10 salams
  • Maliki opinion: 36 rakah total (20 in congregation + 16 during rest periods)
  • All four madhabs: Agree on 20 rakah as the standard
  • Prophetic practice: Evidence suggests Prophet ﷺ prayed 20 rakah during the three nights he led tarawih
